## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Next: spike-validate-workload-wsl-import (₢A-AA3) [rough]

Docket:
  ## Character
  Surgical mechanism-validation spike — one experiment, decisive. Locks the
  shape of the redesign pace.
  
  ## Docket
  Validate that schtasks-as-bujuw_user can run `wsl --import rbtww-main` and
  have the registration land in workload's HKCU\Lxss, so the workload's
  `command=` directive can subsequently resolve `wsl --distribution
  rbtww-main` cleanly.
  
  Shape: set workload password (clears LimitBlankPasswordUse), pre-stage
  rbtww-main tarball at a workload-readable path, schedule a one-shot
  task as workload running `wsl --import`, run the task, probe HKCU\Lxss
  for the new entry.
  
  Open question this spike resolves: does HKCU need pre-loading via a
  one-time interactive logon, or does schtasks-launch handle it?
  microsoft/WSL #10732, #8835, #3918 document run-time WSL failures from
  schtasks without a loaded profile — but those are about RUNNING wsl, not
  IMPORTING. Spike tests import-time specifically.
  
  Done when:
  - HKCU\Lxss probe shows rbtww-main registered for bujuw_user; OR
  - Mechanism fails with enough detail (schtasks error, registry-write
    failure, profile-not-loaded error) to redirect the redesign pace.
  - No garrison-w changes, no BUSJGW redraft, no end-to-end run.

f222dde0  ₢A-AAv    Wrap step 6's workload-knock ssh call with zbujb_validate_run so its stdout and stderr are captured to per-call diag files (bujb_validate_NN_knock-ssh_stdout.txt and _stderr.txt). Pure diagnostic — no fix attempt. Prior run (b431f1c) cleared the silent-preauth gate via profile registration; eventlog-operational confirmed sshd: Accepted publickey for bujuw_user. Step 6 still fails with ssh exit 255, but POST-AUTH — the failure mode shifted from silent-preauth-close to whatever happens during the wsl.exe → bash -lc \"true\" execution that command= directs to. Output capture revealed only a stray T followed by two blank lines in the buc_step preview; the per-call file capture from this wrapping will surface (a) the full bytes the workload session emitted on stdout (Ubuntu WSL profile.d banners? a partial error message?), (b) ssh client's own stderr (the Permission-denied or other exit 255 detail), and (c) confirm whether the knock actually completed cleanly with banner-noise vs failed mid-stream. zbujb_validate_run is the existing per-call capture wrapper used by the other 13 step-6 diag probes (eventlog-operational, sshd-config, getacl-*, etc.); reusing it for the knock-ssh call itself keeps the file-naming convention contiguous (the cycle-8 emit-index counter increments through the knock call before the other probes fire). Wrapper takes the ssh invocation as positional args and redirects via $@. ssh exit code propagates through z_exit unchanged (zbujb_validate_run's || pattern preserves it). bash -n green; not running test suites since change is pure diagnostic instrumentation in step 6 only, no test suite exercises Windows garrison path.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
d65dee17  ₣A-       Add 'Windows: Host Availability (optional)' section to BUK jurisdiction handbook between Linux/Mac note and existing sshd-reachability bootstrap. Covers physically-secured single-occupant rooms that need power-on autonomy: Tailscale install (default per-user auto-start), Run-Unattended mode via tray-icon Preferences (with the issue-#3186 first-boot caveat — interactive login once before unattended mode settles), and Windows auto-login via netplwiz (Win+R, uncheck the password-required checkbox, enter password twice) plus the registry sub-step for Microsoft-account hosts where the checkbox is hidden (set HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\PasswordLess\Device\DevicePasswordLessBuildVersion = 0). Uses buh_step1/buh_step2 macros so step numbering auto-increments and flows into the existing sshd-reachability section (now steps 4-8 instead of 1-5; previously hardcoded). Section is presented as optional with a Precondition block warning that auto-login removes the keyboard barrier (physical access becomes total access). Bootstrap section's Preconditions gains a cross-reference: 'for unattended power-on, see Windows: Host Availability above'. New render fn zbuhj_render_windows_availability called from buhj_top after zbuhj_render_linux_mac_note. No new tabtarget, no zipper entry, no new file — content surfaces automatically through the existing BUWZ_HJ0_TOP reference in the RBK Windows orchestrator's Phase 1.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             
b431f1cd  ₢A-AAv    Codify single-expression PS body discipline (WSG PS-5/6/7), add zbujb_powershell_capture helper, refactor step 3 w-branch profile registration from compound PS to bash-orchestrated single-expression calls. WSG additions: PS-5 (PowerShell bodies are single expressions — bodies are one cmdlet/native binary call, no ;-joined statements with intermediate $var assignments; if N intermediate values needed, run N round-trips and capture in bash), PS-6 (don't interpolate strings via PowerShell when bash can build them — bash builds the literal, PS receives it argument-bound or single-quoted), PS-7 (don't interpolate variables through PowerShell to native binaries — PS argv handling for native Windows binaries has documented quirks with embedded spaces and quotes, use PS cmdlets for PS-native effects or pass already-resolved bash literals). Each WSG rule has anti-pattern + correct-pattern code blocks; PS-5 carries the empirical citation to bujb_jurisdiction.sh:715 pre-decomposition. New helper zbujb_powershell_capture(ROLE, BODY) added next to zbujb_admin_powershell at ~line 380: takes ROLE in {zbujb_privileged, zbujb_workload} dispatching to BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE+BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER vs BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE+BUJB_workload_user respectively, runs single PS expression with same Stop/UTF8/LASTEXITCODE prelude as zbujb_admin_powershell but omits trailing exit-check (capture form preserves stdout), strips Windows CR via bash parameter expansion ${var//$'\r'/} (no tr subprocess), emits stripped stdout via printf '%s' (bash builtin, no echo newline behavior), returns ssh's exit code so caller || buc_die works. local declarations split from $() assignment so local's exit code doesn't mask ssh's. Step 3 w-branch refactored: replaced 4-statement compound PS at line 715 with capture-SID-via-helper + bash-builds-z_regkey + bash-builds-z_homepath + two atomic admin_powershell calls (New-Item then New-ItemProperty). z_homepath uses %SystemDrive%\Users\<wlu> convention matching existing registered profiles in HKLM\...\ProfileList rather than literal C:\Users\<wlu>. Three new local vars declared at function scope (z_sid, z_regkey, z_homepath). bash -n green; not running test suites since change is additive helper + refactor in step 3 w branch only, no test suite exercises Windows garrison path. Hypothesis still under test: profile registration alone (with passwordreq:no + blank-password preserved) clears the silent preauth close, isolating Win32-OpenSSH home-dir-resolution gate from LimitBlankPasswordUse network-logon gate.      
343bfd13  ₢A-AAv    Replace failed reg.exe-based profile registration with PowerShell-native registry cmdlets in step 3 w branch. Prior notch (f8e90c5) used reg.exe via PS, which hit 'ERROR: Invalid syntax' — the registry key path 'HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\ProfileList\<SID>' contains a space ('Windows NT') and PowerShell does not consistently quote args containing spaces when invoking native binaries. New shape uses four ;-joined PS-native statements: (1) $sid=(Get-LocalUser '${z_wlu}').SID.Value resolves the SID; (2) $path='HKLM:\SOFTWARE\...' + $sid concatenates the registry path inside a single PS variable; (3) New-Item $path -Force creates the registry key (idempotent); (4) New-ItemProperty $path -Name 'ProfileImagePath' -Value 'C:\Users\${z_wlu}' -PropertyType ExpandString -Force writes the property. PS-native registry cmdlets accept full paths verbatim — no native-binary argv quirks, spaces handled transparently. Each statement is atomic, no try/catch, no Add-Type, no PS state machine. Per WSG line 322 ;-joined statements remain the proven shape. Pipe to Out-Null on New-Item and New-ItemProperty suppresses cmdlet output objects (avoids PS-2 lazy-formatter risk and keeps stdout clean for the wrapper's exit-check trailer). Bash syntax green; same hypothesis under test as prior notch (profile registration alone clears the silent preauth close).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             
f8e90c53  ₢A-AAv    Add profile registration to step 3 w branch — single zbujb_admin_powershell call after the existing zbujb_admin_exec block, two ;-joined PS statements: (1) $sid=(Get-LocalUser '${z_wlu}').SID.Value resolves the freshly-created workload user's SID; (2) reg.exe add HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\ProfileList\$sid /v ProfileImagePath /t REG_EXPAND_SZ /d C:\Users\${z_wlu} /f writes the minimum-viable profile entry sshd needs for AuthorizedKeysFile home-dir resolution. No Add-Type, no try/catch, no PS state machine — two atomic native-tool/cmdlet calls per WSG line 322 proven shape. Three-line comment explains the why (OpenSSH-Win32 silent preauth close on missing profile registration) and points to Win32-OpenSSH issue #1383 for the citation chain. Hypothesis under test: profile registration alone (with passwordreq:no + blank-password preserved) clears the silent preauth close. If yes — Win32-OpenSSH's home-dir-resolution gate was decisive and LimitBlankPasswordUse=0x1 doesn't apply to our SSH+command= path. If no — also need non-blank password (next cycle). c branch left untouched intentionally; same defect (net.exe user /add /passwordreq:no creates no profile) but fixing one branch at a time per operator's one-experiment-per-cycle discipline. Spec change to BUSJGW deferred until empirical confirmation. Gates: bash -n green; not running test suites since change is additive in step 3 w branch only, no test suite exercises Windows garrison path.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  
10027d66  ₢A-AAv    Add two diagnostic probes to step 6 validate failure path: limit-blank-password (reg query of HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa /v LimitBlankPasswordUse — surfaces whether Windows' default network-logon block on blank-password accounts is in effect) and profile-list (reg query of HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\ProfileList /s — recursively dumps all registered Windows user profiles, surfacing both working baselines and the workload user's missing-or-present state). Both probes follow the existing zbujb_validate_run pattern: single-line atomic call to zbujb_admin_powershell, native-binary body (reg.exe) with stderr swallowed via 2>$null per WSG SH-3 escaping, output materialized via Out-String, suffixed with || true so probe failure doesn't abort the diagnostic chain. Investigation context: prior cycles cleared file-system StrictModes hypotheses (cycle 7 home-dir lockdown landed correctly, cycle-8 instrumentation refactor confirmed); the cycle-8 trial run captured the [preauth] close in OpenSSH/Operational with no Failed-publickey or Authentication-refused line preceding it, ruling out file-permission-walk failures. Off-band probes via tt/buw-jpS this session surfaced two new candidate gates (sshd LocalSystem service account in ACL — ruled out; bujuw_user only in Users group — ruled out; bujuw_user has no Win32_UserProfile or HKLM ProfileList registry entry — confirmed; LimitBlankPasswordUse=0x1 — confirmed enabled; bujuw_user has blank password from net user /add /passwordreq:no — confirmed). Manual repair attempt (set non-blank password on bujuw_user) produced same Permission-denied result; concluded blank-password alone was not the (sole) gate. Profile registration is now the most likely remaining gate per Win32-OpenSSH issue #1383. The two new probes capture this state going forward in committed diagnostic output, so future garrison-w failures (and historical reruns) carry the empirical baseline. Operator framing: 'always debugging something committed' — probe additions land before the next experiment run.

  ## Docket
  Validate that schtasks-as-bujuw_user can run `wsl --import rbtww-main` and
  have the registration land in workload's HKCU\Lxss, so the workload's
  `command=` directive can subsequently resolve `wsl --distribution
  rbtww-main` cleanly.
  
  Shape: set workload password (clears LimitBlankPasswordUse), pre-stage
  rbtww-main tarball at a workload-readable path, schedule a one-shot
  task as workload running `wsl --import`, run the task, probe HKCU\Lxss
  for the new entry.
  
  Open question this spike resolves: does HKCU need pre-loading via a
  one-time interactive logon, or does schtasks-launch handle it?
  microsoft/WSL #10732, #8835, #3918 document run-time WSL failures from
  schtasks without a loaded profile — but those are about RUNNING wsl, not
  IMPORTING. Spike tests import-time specifically.
  
  Done when:
  - HKCU\Lxss probe shows rbtww-main registered for bujuw_user; OR
  - Mechanism fails with enough detail (schtasks error, registry-write
    failure, profile-not-loaded error) to redirect the redesign pace.
  - No garrison-w changes, no BUSJGW redraft, no end-to-end run.

content.content:
1	# jjezs_paddock ₣A-
2	
3	## Goal
4	
5	Windows test infrastructure for Recipe Bottle. Run RB's container isolation
6	tests on a Windows host with WSL, Cygwin, and PowerShell access paths.
7	Container runtime for release-1 is Docker Desktop for Windows only; the
8	WSL distro `rbtww-main` consumes Docker Desktop's per-distro WSL
9	integration rather than running its own dockerd. Native dockerd inside
10	WSL is named in §Deferred for the shared-kernel-iptables reason.
11	
12	## Current Concept
13	
14	**Jurisdiction** is the BUK feature-area for reaching out to remote nodes
15	deterministically. Logical-only umbrella concept; `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` is
16	its implementation seat (BCG-compliant module housing both verbs). Neither
17	the umbrella nor the implementation module earns AXLA-entity voicing in
18	BUS0. Architectural spine in BUS0 §Remote Node Access.
19	
20	**Two regimes (BURN, BURP):**
21	
22	- BURN — node-shape (host, OS family). Per-node, git-transported, changes
23	  rarely. Identifier: investiture.
24	- BURP — privileged + workload credentials. Per-station-user. Three fields:
25	  `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER`, `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`,
26	  `BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE`. Pubkeys derived from privkeys via
27	  `ssh-keygen -y` at use-time; no separate pubkey paths or inline strings.
28	  Identifier: investiture — by construction equal to a registered
29	  investiture. The BURP directory name must match a BURN profile dir;
30	  the 1:1 correspondence is enforced by file-presence check at load time.
31	
32	**BUJB constant:**
33	
34	- `BUJB_workload_user` — project-wide convention name for the workload OS
35	  user that garrison provisions on every node. Single source of truth.
36	  Lives in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` as a tinder constant.
37	
38	**Two verbs (Fenestrate, Garrison):**
39	
40	- **Fenestrate** — Windows-OpenSSH-only `sshd_config` harden plus admin SSH
41	  key-trust establishment. Uniform across shell-letters (no variant).
42	  Two-phase ceremony forced by `Restart-Service sshd` killing its own
43	  session. Tabtarget `buw-jpF` (capital F = persistent config application).
44	- **Garrison** — workload account provisioning only. Per-shell-letter
45	  (b/c/w). Never reads or writes any `sshd_config`; never alters admin
46	  authorized_keys. First action is admin SSH via key auth; failure surfaces
47	  immediately.
48	- For Linux/Mac there is no fenestrate verb — admin trust is operator-manual
49	  (e.g., `ssh-copy-id`); garrison runs against an existing key-trusted admin
50	  foothold. For Cygwin/WSL sshd hardening on Windows nodes (in addition to
51	  Windows OpenSSH), hardening is operator-manual.
52	
53	**Garrison-destructive model:**
54	
55	- Every garrison wipes prior workload state regardless of which investiture
56	  or operator runs it. Node runtime state belongs to whoever last garrisoned.
57	- One workload user per node, ever. No conscript / discharge / withdraw /
58	  vanquish / inventory verbs; garrison subsumes them.
59	- Admin-side sshd configuration is fenestrate's scope on Windows;
60	  operator-manual elsewhere.
61	
62	**Cross-cutting premises:**
63	
64	- `burn_host_singularity` — host of a node lives only in BURN; BURP reaches
65	  it by reference, never duplicates.
66	- `bus_keys_operator_owned` — system never generates or modifies SSH key
67	  material; operator owns all key administration.
68	
69	**Tabtarget colophons (14 total; b/c/w shell support, PowerShell deferred):**
70	
71	- `buw-r[np][lrv]` — BURN/BURP regime config (List/Render/Validate) — 6
72	- `buw-jpF` — Fenestrate (Windows OpenSSH only; uniform) — 1
73	- `buw-jpG[bcw]` — Garrison per shell-letter (b=native bash for Linux/Mac;
74	  c=Cygwin; w=WSL) — 3
75	- `buw-jwk` — Knock (probe workload reachability) — 1
76	- `buw-jwc` — Run command file as workload (shell determined by garrison) — 1
77	- `buw-jws` — Interactive SSH as workload — 1
78	- `buw-hj0` — Handbook jurisdiction top — 1
79	
80	**Operational seat (`bujb_jurisdiction.sh` + `bujb_cli.sh`):**
81	
82	BCG-compliant module housing both fenestrate and garrison implementations,
83	hardcoding the three shell-letter→`command=` directive mappings (b/c/w)
84	used by garrison on the workload account, the workload privkey destination
85	path on remote per shell-letter, the canonical WSL distribution name
86	(`rbtww-main`), and the Windows OpenSSH `sshd_config` hardening directive
87	set used by fenestrate. SSH transport mechanics live in code; BURP carries
88	no shell selection.
89	
90	**Fenestrate ceremony (Windows OpenSSH only; uniform):**
91	
92	Two-phase remote-run because `Restart-Service sshd` terminates its own
93	ssh session.
94	
95	Phase 1 (over the password-or-key admin SSH session):
96	
97	1. SSH-as-`BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with
98	   `PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password`. Steady-state:
99	   `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE` authenticates by key. First-run: ssh prompts
100	   on /dev/tty; operator types the Windows admin password once. Fenestrate
101	   process never sees the password.
102	2. Idempotently place admin pubkey (derived from `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`
103	   via `ssh-keygen -y`) in `administrators_authorized_keys` as a bare entry
104	   (no `command=`; fenestrate is uniform). Performed before any sshd_config
105	   change so key auth is available for phase 2's reconnect.
106	3. `icacls` lockdown on `administrators_authorized_keys`.
107	4. Write hardened directives to `sshd_config`: `PubkeyAuthentication yes`,
108	   `PasswordAuthentication no`, `PermitEmptyPasswords no`. Idempotent.
109	5. Verify written state via PowerShell `Get-Content` returning raw bytes,
110	   then bash-side BCG-compliant matching extracting effective directive
111	   values. Keeps PowerShell out of parsing (its error semantics are
112	   unreliable).
113	6. `sshd -t` validates the config (penultimate atomic op of phase 1; a
114	   malformed config aborts fenestrate before it could brick running sshd).
115	7. `Restart-Service sshd` (final atomic op of phase 1; ssh session
116	   terminates here — bash orchestrator treats the disconnect as the
117	   expected phase boundary).
118	
119	Phase 2 (key auth only):
120	
121	8. Reconnect as `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with
122	   `PreferredAuthentications=publickey` (no password fallback). Failure
123	   here surfaces a brick — the operator's manual password no longer opens
124	   a session.
125	9. Verify post-state: the admin session authenticates by key alone and the
126	   running sshd serves the hardened configuration.
127	
128	**Garrison ceremony (per shell-letter; workload-only):**
129	
130	1. SSH-as-`BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`; key auth
131	   only (no password fallback). On Windows, presupposes fenestrate has run
132	   for this investiture. On Linux/Mac, admin trust is operator-managed
133	   (e.g., `ssh-copy-id` placed the admin pubkey).
134	2. Destroy existing workload user (if present) via `userdel -r` or platform
135	   equivalent — removes account + home directory; stray files outside home
136	   are workload's own concern, not garrison's.
137	3. Create fresh workload user named `BUJB_workload_user` (unprivileged: no
138	   sudo, no admin group, ssh-only access).
139	4. Place workload pubkey in workload's authorized_keys with the
140	   shell-letter's `command=` directive.
141	5. Copy workload privkey to remote workload account at the path hardcoded in
142	   `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` per shell-letter (so workload can authenticate
143	   outbound to GitHub).
144	6. Validate round-trip — SSH as workload + exec no-op + verify exit 0.
145	
146	## Heat Sequence
147	
148	**Spec foundation:**
149	- spec-bus0-jurisdiction — BUS0 rewrite reflecting current concept
150	- introduce-fenestrate-narrow-garrison — split admin trust + sshd harden
151	  out of garrison into a uniform fenestrate verb
152	
153	**Implementation:**
154	- implement-jurisdiction — four-field BURP, `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER`,
155	  `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` module, 14 tabtargets, fenestrate + garrison
156	  ceremonies, cleanup superseded colophons + handbook entries
157	
158	**Investigation (independent, runs anytime):**
159	- localhost-fundus-parallel-saturation diagnosis
160	
161	**Practice (Windows host, blocks on implementation):**
162	- WSL distribution install (`rbtww-main` set as default)
163	- Garrison-WSL exercise
164	- Garrison-Cygwin exercise
165	- Docker Desktop WSL integration enable + verify (no native dockerd)
166	
167	## Deferred (named for future return)
168	
169	- **PowerShell garrison + dispatch** — `buw-jpGp` tabtarget + the `p`
170	  shell-letter mapping in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. Current heat ships b/c/w
171	  only.
172	- **Adopt** — cross-station-user pubkey install via existing privileged trust.
173	  Today: each station user runs the ceremony fresh from their own machine.
174	- **User regime** — centralized per-user pubkey for shared admin scenarios.
175	- **Separate workload→GitHub keypair** — current shape reuses workload SSH
176	  keypair for outbound; tighten to per-purpose separation if security demands.
177	- **BURP override fields for non-conventional key layouts** — operators with
178	  centralized SSH key storage, non-`.pub`-suffix layouts.
179	- **Localhost garrison/dispatch variants** — deferred; remote nodes only.
180	- **Git normalization on remote** — JJK has `jjx_plant`; BUK doesn't duplicate.
181	- **Cygwin / WSL sshd hardening verb** — fenestrate covers Windows OpenSSH
182	  only. If c/w garrisons ever require their own sshd to be hardened
183	  programmatically (rather than operator-managed), that is a future
184	  fenestrate sibling.
185	- **Native dockerd inside `rbtww-main`** — Docker Desktop and a per-distro
186	  native daemon cannot safely coexist on the same Windows host: WSL2 distros
187	  share one network namespace, so two daemons fight for kernel iptables/NAT
188	  rules. Release-1 ships Docker Desktop only; Desktop's WSL integration
189	  serves the rbtww-main shells. If isolation needs ever require a native
190	  daemon, it would be operator-managed exclusive lifecycle (one daemon at
191	  a time, never both running concurrently).
192	- **JJK fundus user provisioning under WSL** — fundus is a JJK concept and
193	  currently deferred; the Windows handbook does not include a fundus phase.
194	
195	## Standing Notes
196	
197	- **Fenestrate handles first-time admin trust establishment on Windows.**
198	  On a fresh node, ssh's `PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password` falls
199	  through to a /dev/tty password prompt; operator types the Windows admin
200	  password once. Fenestrate then installs the admin pubkey, sets the icacls
201	  ACL, hardens sshd_config (`PasswordAuthentication no`), restarts sshd,
202	  and reconnects to verify by key. Subsequent runs use key auth
203	  automatically. The operator's manual Windows scope reduces to: install
204	  OpenSSH server, enable the service, set `PasswordAuthentication yes`
205	  temporarily, allow the firewall port, ensure the admin user has a known
206	  password.
207	- **Linux/Mac admin trust is operator-managed.** No fenestrate verb;
208	  operator places admin pubkey via `ssh-copy-id` (or equivalent), and
209	  garrison's first admin SSH succeeds by key alone.
210	- **Two-phase fenestrate is structural, not optional.** `Restart-Service
211	  sshd` kills its own ssh session, so any post-restart verification must
212	  happen over a fresh session. Bash orchestrator treats the post-restart
213	  disconnect as the expected phase boundary, not a failure.
214	- **Encrypted (passphrase-protected) privkeys not supported.** `ssh-keygen -y`
215	  prompts interactively; fenestrate and garrison would hang. Operator
216	  policy: privkeys unencrypted; station-level security covers
217	  privkey-at-rest protection.
218	- **Workload pubkey must be pre-registered with GitHub out-of-band.** Operator
219	  pastes the pubkey into GitHub (deploy key per repo or personal SSH key)
220	  before garrison runs. Garrison cannot validate this; it is a precondition.
221	- **WSL distribution is hardcoded to `rbtww-main`** in
222	  `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. If the current default WSL distribution differs,
223	  garrison-WSL fails fast with a clear error.
224	- **Docker Desktop is the release-1 container runtime on Windows.** WSL
225	  shells reach the daemon via Docker Desktop's per-distro WSL integration
226	  toggle (Settings → Resources → WSL Integration), enabled for `rbtww-main`.
227	  Cygwin and Windows-side shells reach the same daemon via the default
228	  Docker context. No separate dockerd inside the WSL distro; only one
229	  daemon ever runs concurrently.
230	- **Some `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` values may contain spaces** (e.g., Windows
231	  admin user `b hyslop`); audit shell quoting at every variable expansion and
232	  path construction.
233	- Tailscale provides stable transport (Mac sees `rocket` as `100.x.y.z` /
234	  tailnet hostname); not adopted as a BUK dependency.
235	- PowerShell-from-WSL pattern: bash owns control flow, PowerShell is the
236	  Windows syscall layer; one atomic command per call.
237	- `command=` in authorized_keys is OpenSSH-standard (used by gitolite, GitHub,
238	  rsync, borg); shrinks key blast radius rather than expanding it.
239	
240	# jjezs_pace ₢A-AA3
241	
242	## Character
243	Surgical mechanism-validation spike — one experiment, decisive. Locks the
244	shape of the redesign pace.
245	
246	## Docket
247	Validate that schtasks-as-bujuw_user can run `wsl --import rbtww-main` and
248	have the registration land in workload's HKCU\Lxss, so the workload's
249	`command=` directive can subsequently resolve `wsl --distribution
250	rbtww-main` cleanly.
251	
252	Shape: set workload password (clears LimitBlankPasswordUse), pre-stage
253	rbtww-main tarball at a workload-readable path, schedule a one-shot
254	task as workload running `wsl --import`, run the task, probe HKCU\Lxss
255	for the new entry.
256	
257	Open question this spike resolves: does HKCU need pre-loading via a
258	one-time interactive logon, or does schtasks-launch handle it?
259	microsoft/WSL #10732, #8835, #3918 document run-time WSL failures from
260	schtasks without a loaded profile — but those are about RUNNING wsl, not
261	IMPORTING. Spike tests import-time specifically.
262	
263	Done when:
264	- HKCU\Lxss probe shows rbtww-main registered for bujuw_user; OR
265	- Mechanism fails with enough detail (schtasks error, registry-write
266	  failure, profile-not-loaded error) to redirect the redesign pace.
267	- No garrison-w changes, no BUSJGW redraft, no end-to-end run.

# jjezs_paddock ₣A-

## Goal

Windows test infrastructure for Recipe Bottle. Run RB's container isolation
tests on a Windows host with WSL, Cygwin, and PowerShell access paths.
Container runtime for release-1 is Docker Desktop for Windows only; the
WSL distro `rbtww-main` consumes Docker Desktop's per-distro WSL
integration rather than running its own dockerd. Native dockerd inside
WSL is named in §Deferred for the shared-kernel-iptables reason.

## Current Concept

**Jurisdiction** is the BUK feature-area for reaching out to remote nodes
deterministically. Logical-only umbrella concept; `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` is
its implementation seat (BCG-compliant module housing both verbs). Neither
the umbrella nor the implementation module earns AXLA-entity voicing in
BUS0. Architectural spine in BUS0 §Remote Node Access.

**Two regimes (BURN, BURP):**

- BURN — node-shape (host, OS family). Per-node, git-transported, changes
  rarely. Identifier: investiture.
- BURP — privileged + workload credentials. Per-station-user. Three fields:
  `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER`, `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`,
  `BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE`. Pubkeys derived from privkeys via
  `ssh-keygen -y` at use-time; no separate pubkey paths or inline strings.
  Identifier: investiture — by construction equal to a registered
  investiture. The BURP directory name must match a BURN profile dir;
  the 1:1 correspondence is enforced by file-presence check at load time.

**BUJB constant:**

- `BUJB_workload_user` — project-wide convention name for the workload OS
  user that garrison provisions on every node. Single source of truth.
  Lives in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` as a tinder constant.

**Two verbs (Fenestrate, Garrison):**

- **Fenestrate** — Windows-OpenSSH-only `sshd_config` harden plus admin SSH
  key-trust establishment. Uniform across shell-letters (no variant).
  Two-phase ceremony forced by `Restart-Service sshd` killing its own
  session. Tabtarget `buw-jpF` (capital F = persistent config application).
- **Garrison** — workload account provisioning only. Per-shell-letter
  (b/c/w). Never reads or writes any `sshd_config`; never alters admin
  authorized_keys. First action is admin SSH via key auth; failure surfaces
  immediately.
- For Linux/Mac there is no fenestrate verb — admin trust is operator-manual
  (e.g., `ssh-copy-id`); garrison runs against an existing key-trusted admin
  foothold. For Cygwin/WSL sshd hardening on Windows nodes (in addition to
  Windows OpenSSH), hardening is operator-manual.

**Garrison-destructive model:**

- Every garrison wipes prior workload state regardless of which investiture
  or operator runs it. Node runtime state belongs to whoever last garrisoned.
- One workload user per node, ever. No conscript / discharge / withdraw /
  vanquish / inventory verbs; garrison subsumes them.
- Admin-side sshd configuration is fenestrate's scope on Windows;
  operator-manual elsewhere.

**Cross-cutting premises:**

- `burn_host_singularity` — host of a node lives only in BURN; BURP reaches
  it by reference, never duplicates.
- `bus_keys_operator_owned` — system never generates or modifies SSH key
  material; operator owns all key administration.

**Tabtarget colophons (14 total; b/c/w shell support, PowerShell deferred):**

- `buw-r[np][lrv]` — BURN/BURP regime config (List/Render/Validate) — 6
- `buw-jpF` — Fenestrate (Windows OpenSSH only; uniform) — 1
- `buw-jpG[bcw]` — Garrison per shell-letter (b=native bash for Linux/Mac;
  c=Cygwin; w=WSL) — 3
- `buw-jwk` — Knock (probe workload reachability) — 1
- `buw-jwc` — Run command file as workload (shell determined by garrison) — 1
- `buw-jws` — Interactive SSH as workload — 1
- `buw-hj0` — Handbook jurisdiction top — 1

**Operational seat (`bujb_jurisdiction.sh` + `bujb_cli.sh`):**

BCG-compliant module housing both fenestrate and garrison implementations,
hardcoding the three shell-letter→`command=` directive mappings (b/c/w)
used by garrison on the workload account, the workload privkey destination
path on remote per shell-letter, the canonical WSL distribution name
(`rbtww-main`), and the Windows OpenSSH `sshd_config` hardening directive
set used by fenestrate. SSH transport mechanics live in code; BURP carries
no shell selection.

**Fenestrate ceremony (Windows OpenSSH only; uniform):**

Two-phase remote-run because `Restart-Service sshd` terminates its own
ssh session.

Phase 1 (over the password-or-key admin SSH session):

1. SSH-as-`BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with
   `PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password`. Steady-state:
   `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE` authenticates by key. First-run: ssh prompts
   on /dev/tty; operator types the Windows admin password once. Fenestrate
   process never sees the password.
2. Idempotently place admin pubkey (derived from `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`
   via `ssh-keygen -y`) in `administrators_authorized_keys` as a bare entry
   (no `command=`; fenestrate is uniform). Performed before any sshd_config
   change so key auth is available for phase 2's reconnect.
3. `icacls` lockdown on `administrators_authorized_keys`.
4. Write hardened directives to `sshd_config`: `PubkeyAuthentication yes`,
   `PasswordAuthentication no`, `PermitEmptyPasswords no`. Idempotent.
5. Verify written state via PowerShell `Get-Content` returning raw bytes,
   then bash-side BCG-compliant matching extracting effective directive
   values. Keeps PowerShell out of parsing (its error semantics are
   unreliable).
6. `sshd -t` validates the config (penultimate atomic op of phase 1; a
   malformed config aborts fenestrate before it could brick running sshd).
7. `Restart-Service sshd` (final atomic op of phase 1; ssh session
   terminates here — bash orchestrator treats the disconnect as the
   expected phase boundary).

Phase 2 (key auth only):

8. Reconnect as `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with
   `PreferredAuthentications=publickey` (no password fallback). Failure
   here surfaces a brick — the operator's manual password no longer opens
   a session.
9. Verify post-state: the admin session authenticates by key alone and the
   running sshd serves the hardened configuration.

**Garrison ceremony (per shell-letter; workload-only):**

1. SSH-as-`BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`; key auth
   only (no password fallback). On Windows, presupposes fenestrate has run
   for this investiture. On Linux/Mac, admin trust is operator-managed
   (e.g., `ssh-copy-id` placed the admin pubkey).
2. Destroy existing workload user (if present) via `userdel -r` or platform
   equivalent — removes account + home directory; stray files outside home
   are workload's own concern, not garrison's.
3. Create fresh workload user named `BUJB_workload_user` (unprivileged: no
   sudo, no admin group, ssh-only access).
4. Place workload pubkey in workload's authorized_keys with the
   shell-letter's `command=` directive.
5. Copy workload privkey to remote workload account at the path hardcoded in
   `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` per shell-letter (so workload can authenticate
   outbound to GitHub).
6. Validate round-trip — SSH as workload + exec no-op + verify exit 0.

## Heat Sequence

**Spec foundation:**
- spec-bus0-jurisdiction — BUS0 rewrite reflecting current concept
- introduce-fenestrate-narrow-garrison — split admin trust + sshd harden
  out of garrison into a uniform fenestrate verb

**Implementation:**
- implement-jurisdiction — four-field BURP, `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER`,
  `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` module, 14 tabtargets, fenestrate + garrison
  ceremonies, cleanup superseded colophons + handbook entries

**Investigation (independent, runs anytime):**
- localhost-fundus-parallel-saturation diagnosis

**Practice (Windows host, blocks on implementation):**
- WSL distribution install (`rbtww-main` set as default)
- Garrison-WSL exercise
- Garrison-Cygwin exercise
- Docker Desktop WSL integration enable + verify (no native dockerd)

## Deferred (named for future return)

- **PowerShell garrison + dispatch** — `buw-jpGp` tabtarget + the `p`
  shell-letter mapping in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. Current heat ships b/c/w
  only.
- **Adopt** — cross-station-user pubkey install via existing privileged trust.
  Today: each station user runs the ceremony fresh from their own machine.
- **User regime** — centralized per-user pubkey for shared admin scenarios.
- **Separate workload→GitHub keypair** — current shape reuses workload SSH
  keypair for outbound; tighten to per-purpose separation if security demands.
- **BURP override fields for non-conventional key layouts** — operators with
  centralized SSH key storage, non-`.pub`-suffix layouts.
- **Localhost garrison/dispatch variants** — deferred; remote nodes only.
- **Git normalization on remote** — JJK has `jjx_plant`; BUK doesn't duplicate.
- **Cygwin / WSL sshd hardening verb** — fenestrate covers Windows OpenSSH
  only. If c/w garrisons ever require their own sshd to be hardened
  programmatically (rather than operator-managed), that is a future
  fenestrate sibling.
- **Native dockerd inside `rbtww-main`** — Docker Desktop and a per-distro
  native daemon cannot safely coexist on the same Windows host: WSL2 distros
  share one network namespace, so two daemons fight for kernel iptables/NAT
  rules. Release-1 ships Docker Desktop only; Desktop's WSL integration
  serves the rbtww-main shells. If isolation needs ever require a native
  daemon, it would be operator-managed exclusive lifecycle (one daemon at
  a time, never both running concurrently).
- **JJK fundus user provisioning under WSL** — fundus is a JJK concept and
  currently deferred; the Windows handbook does not include a fundus phase.

## Standing Notes

- **Fenestrate handles first-time admin trust establishment on Windows.**
  On a fresh node, ssh's `PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password` falls
  through to a /dev/tty password prompt; operator types the Windows admin
  password once. Fenestrate then installs the admin pubkey, sets the icacls
  ACL, hardens sshd_config (`PasswordAuthentication no`), restarts sshd,
  and reconnects to verify by key. Subsequent runs use key auth
  automatically. The operator's manual Windows scope reduces to: install
  OpenSSH server, enable the service, set `PasswordAuthentication yes`
  temporarily, allow the firewall port, ensure the admin user has a known
  password.
- **Linux/Mac admin trust is operator-managed.** No fenestrate verb;
  operator places admin pubkey via `ssh-copy-id` (or equivalent), and
  garrison's first admin SSH succeeds by key alone.
- **Two-phase fenestrate is structural, not optional.** `Restart-Service
  sshd` kills its own ssh session, so any post-restart verification must
  happen over a fresh session. Bash orchestrator treats the post-restart
  disconnect as the expected phase boundary, not a failure.
- **Encrypted (passphrase-protected) privkeys not supported.** `ssh-keygen -y`
  prompts interactively; fenestrate and garrison would hang. Operator
  policy: privkeys unencrypted; station-level security covers
  privkey-at-rest protection.
- **Workload pubkey must be pre-registered with GitHub out-of-band.** Operator
  pastes the pubkey into GitHub (deploy key per repo or personal SSH key)
  before garrison runs. Garrison cannot validate this; it is a precondition.
- **WSL distribution is hardcoded to `rbtww-main`** in
  `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. If the current default WSL distribution differs,
  garrison-WSL fails fast with a clear error.
- **Docker Desktop is the release-1 container runtime on Windows.** WSL
  shells reach the daemon via Docker Desktop's per-distro WSL integration
  toggle (Settings → Resources → WSL Integration), enabled for `rbtww-main`.
  Cygwin and Windows-side shells reach the same daemon via the default
  Docker context. No separate dockerd inside the WSL distro; only one
  daemon ever runs concurrently.
- **Some `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` values may contain spaces** (e.g., Windows
  admin user `b hyslop`); audit shell quoting at every variable expansion and
  path construction.
- Tailscale provides stable transport (Mac sees `rocket` as `100.x.y.z` /
  tailnet hostname); not adopted as a BUK dependency.
- PowerShell-from-WSL pattern: bash owns control flow, PowerShell is the
  Windows syscall layer; one atomic command per call.
- `command=` in authorized_keys is OpenSSH-standard (used by gitolite, GitHub,
  rsync, borg); shrinks key blast radius rather than expanding it.

# jjezs_pace ₢A-AA3

## Character
Surgical mechanism-validation spike — one experiment, decisive. Locks the
shape of the redesign pace.

## Docket
Validate that schtasks-as-bujuw_user can run `wsl --import rbtww-main` and
have the registration land in workload's HKCU\Lxss, so the workload's
`command=` directive can subsequently resolve `wsl --distribution
rbtww-main` cleanly.

Shape: set workload password (clears LimitBlankPasswordUse), pre-stage
rbtww-main tarball at a workload-readable path, schedule a one-shot
task as workload running `wsl --import`, run the task, probe HKCU\Lxss
for the new entry.

Open question this spike resolves: does HKCU need pre-loading via a
one-time interactive logon, or does schtasks-launch handle it?
microsoft/WSL #10732, #8835, #3918 document run-time WSL failures from
schtasks without a loaded profile — but those are about RUNNING wsl, not
IMPORTING. Spike tests import-time specifically.

Done when:
- HKCU\Lxss probe shows rbtww-main registered for bujuw_user; OR
- Mechanism fails with enough detail (schtasks error, registry-write
  failure, profile-not-loaded error) to redirect the redesign pace.
- No garrison-w changes, no BUSJGW redraft, no end-to-end run.

content.text:
## ₢A-AA3 — spike-validate-workload-wsl-import

**Pace goal**: Run a single decisive experiment to confirm whether a `schtasks`-launched `wsl --import rbtww-main` executing as `bujuw_user` registers the distribution in the workload's `HKCU\Lxss`. The answer locks the shape of the upcoming redesign pace (workload-owns-WSL-distribution).

### Heat context
**₣A- — rbk-mvp-3-draft-windows-procedures** (22/29 done) — Windows test infrastructure for RB. The relevant arc: prior cycles on ₢A-AAv chased step-6 silent preauth, identified missing Windows profile registration as the gate (via Win32-OpenSSH #1383), landed the PowerShell New-Item/New-ItemProperty fix — auth now succeeds. The post-auth failure then exposed a deeper architectural issue: WSL is per-user by Microsoft design, and `rbtww-main` was admin-installed, so `bujuw_user`'s `HKCU\Lxss` is empty. The workload cannot resolve `wsl --distribution rbtww-main` because the distro doesn't exist in its registry namespace. **Architectural finding**: the workload must OWN its own WSL distribution; BUSJGW's two-namespace mirrored-identity framing didn't anticipate WSL distribution registration as a third namespace.

This pace is the spike that validates the mechanism (schtasks → `wsl --import` → HKCU registration) before committing to the redesign.

### Docket (full)
- **Shape**: set workload password (clears `LimitBlankPasswordUse`), pre-stage `rbtww-main` tarball at a workload-readable path, schedule a one-shot schtasks running `wsl --import` as workload, run the task, probe `HKCU\Lxss` for the new entry.
- **Open question this spike resolves**: does HKCU need pre-loading via a one-time interactive logon, or does schtasks-launch handle it? microsoft/WSL #10732, #8835, #3918 document run-time WSL failures from schtasks without a loaded profile — but those are about RUNNING wsl, not IMPORTING. Spike tests import-time specifically.
- **Done when**: HKCU\Lxss probe shows `rbtww-main` registered for `bujuw_user`; OR mechanism fails with enough detail (schtasks error, registry-write failure, profile-not-loaded error) to redirect the redesign pace.
- **Out of scope**: no garrison-w changes, no BUSJGW redraft, no end-to-end run.

### Silks assessment
`spike-validate-workload-wsl-import` matches the docket cleanly — no rename needed.

### Proposed approach
- **New diagnostic tabtarget** (likely `buw-jpVw.SpikeWorkloadWslImport.sh` or similar — keeps the spike orthogonal from `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`, deletable when redesign lands). Standalone diagnostic, doesn't touch garrison flow.
- **Tarball source**: `wsl --export rbtww-main <path>` from the privileged session (workload-readable path under `C:\Users\Public\` or workload's own profile dir).
- **Workload password set**: piggyback the cycle-9 pattern — `net user bujuw_user <password>` via `zbujb_admin_powershell`. The current `passwordreq:no` blank state is what `LimitBlankPasswordUse=0x1` blocks for network logon; schtasks needs a real password to launch a process as the user.
- **Schtasks shape**: `schtasks /create /tn rbtww-import-spike /tr "wsl --import rbtww-main C:\Users\bujuw_user\rbtww-fs <tarball>" /sc once /st <time> /ru bujuw_user /rp <password>` then `/run` then `/delete`. Single PS expression per WSG PS-5; bash builds the strings per WSG PS-6.
- **Probe**: `reg query HKU\<bujuw_user_SID>\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Lxss /s` from the privileged session — HKU access from admin lets us read the workload's hive without needing a workload SSH session. Capture full output for the diagnostic record.

### Execution strategy
**Sequential, opus.** This is a single-file additive change (one new tabtarget script) with high WSG-discipline sensitivity (PS-5/6/7 newly codified, native-binary argv quirks, schtasks's own `/rp` quoting). One operator, one experiment, one decisive output. No parallelism needed.

Ready to proceed?

message.content: Hmm, I'm skeptical of this schtasks thing.

content.text:
Fair pushback. Schtasks is the heavy mechanism — async scheduler, password on `/rp` command line, task cleanup, and the very issues the docket cites (#10732, #8835, #3918) are about scheduler-launched wsl not having HKCU loaded. The bet that import is different from running is exactly what we'd be testing, but with a mechanism that's already on shaky ground.

**Alternative: SSH-as-workload with a spike-only authorized_keys entry.**

Workload SSH auth already works post-cycle-9. The current post-auth failure is that the workload's `command=` directive points at `wsl --distribution rbtww-main bash -lc ...` which fails because rbtww-main isn't in workload's HKCU\Lxss — but that failure mode tells us the SSH session itself is opening as a real bujuw_user logon. We just can't get to a useful prompt because the existing command= directs at the broken wsl path.

Spike shape:
1. Privileged session installs a second authorized_keys line for bujuw_user with `command="wsl.exe --import rbtww-main C:\Users\bujuw_user\rbtww-fs C:\Users\Public\rbtww.tar"` plus restrictions
2. Pre-stage tarball via privileged `wsl --export rbtww-main C:\Users\Public\rbtww.tar`
3. SSH as bujuw_user using the spike key — command= fires the import in a real workload logon session
4. From privileged session, `reg query HKU\<bujuw_SID>\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Lxss /s` to probe

Why this beats schtasks: SSH-as-workload is a synchronous network logon that Windows fully tokenizes (we just spent four cycles proving that); empirical mechanism is the same channel the eventual garrison redesign would naturally use; no scheduler indirection, no /rp password-in-clear, no async poll. If `wsl --import` writes to HKCU\Lxss in a real workload logon session, this surfaces it immediately. If it doesn't write to HKCU here, schtasks wasn't going to do better.

Want me to reslate the docket toward the SSH-driven shape, or is there a reason to keep schtasks in the running?

message.content: We need to discuss until the approach is simple.  You are amplifying noise.  What is our good simple next step?

content.text:
Strip it to: do the experiment by hand.

From the privileged session we already have working: temporarily drop the `command=` from bujuw_user's authorized_keys, SSH in as workload, run `wsl --import rbtww-main <path> <tarball>`, `reg query HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Lxss`, restore the command=. No new tabtarget, no spike script, no schtasks. Four commands, one yes/no answer.

If the answer is yes, the redesign pace knows the mechanism. If no, the redesign pace knows the deeper Windows constraint and we adjust.

lastPrompt: We need to discuss until the approach is simple.  You are amplifying noise.  What is our good simple next step?

message.content: Chuckle, sigh.  I have been 'starving'

Chuckle, sigh.  I have been 'starving' this chat of attention, hoping your next inclinations would lead towards solution, but I'm afraid I'm less confident you are on a convergent path.  Lets reconsider what we are trying here- I think your last finding was important.  But we have decisions to make, I never liked the valence of the schtasks, preferring something more 'deterministic' and repeatable.  Help me get back in sync with your approach given our goal of a really clear workload member getting set up with garrison.

content.text:
You're right — let me re-anchor.

**The goal**: `tt/buw-jpGw.GarrisonWsl.sh` runs end-to-end and produces a Windows node where the workload account, when SSH'd into with `command="wsl --distribution rbtww-main bash -lc ..."`, executes that shell cleanly. Repeatable from any privileged station, against any Windows node.

**What we know forces the redesign**: WSL distributions are per-user (Microsoft, by design). The privileged user's `rbtww-main` is invisible to `bujuw_user`. So garrison-w must provision a WSL distribution *under bujuw_user's HKCU* — not just provision a workload OS account.

**The one decision that matters**: how does garrison cause `wsl --import rbtww-main` to execute as bujuw_user from the privileged orchestrator?

The deterministic, repeatable, already-proven channel is **SSH-as-workload**. We just spent four cycles proving Win32-OpenSSH builds a real workload logon session for bujuw_user. That session IS Windows' canonical "run something as this user" — synchronous, exit-code-honest, no scheduler indirection, no `/rp` password on the cmdline, no async polling. It's the same channel garrison already uses to copy the workload privkey and validate round-trip.

**The shape this implies for garrison-w**:

The current step ordering can't work — step 4 places authorized_keys with the wsl-bound `command=`, then step 5 SSH's as workload (which now fails because wsl can't resolve rbtww-main). So garrison-w grows a setup-then-lockdown pattern:

1. Place workload pubkey with **bare** authorized_keys (no command=)
2. SSH-as-workload: `wsl --import rbtww-main <workload-path> <tarball>`, copy privkey, validate
3. Replace authorized_keys with the production locked-down `command=` entry

The "spike" then collapses: it's just step 2 of the new shape, run once and probed. We don't need a separate spike tabtarget — the spike IS the redesign's first execution, with one extra `reg query HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Lxss` to confirm registration landed.

**What this asks of you**:
- Confirm "workload owns its WSL via `wsl --import` from a baseline tarball" is the redesign you want (vs. e.g., `wsl --install <distro>` from the Store, or sidestepping WSL for workload entirely in favor of Cygwin).
- Confirm the bare-then-lockdown authorized_keys ordering is acceptable (it's a brief security window during garrison itself, between provisioning and final lockdown — not a steady-state weakening).

If both yes, then the next pace stops being a spike and becomes the redesign itself, and the three remaining heat paces (₢A-AA3, ₢A-AA2, ₢A-AA1) collapse meaningfully.

message.content: I might not be fully aligned, but the 'garrison' sequence can trigger the workload identity to set up its wsl precisely and controlled, right?  That seems very good to me as an approach; what do you think?

content.text:
Yes — that's exactly the right framing, and I think it's good.

Garrison is the **conductor**: it owns the sequence, the tarball provenance, the path conventions, the validation gates. But it doesn't try to *be* the workload. It opens an SSH-as-workload channel and tells the workload's own logon session "run `wsl --import` now" — the work happens in bujuw_user's identity, the registration lands in bujuw_user's HKCU, and garrison observes the exit code and probes the result.

This is conceptually cleaner than schtasks because the identity boundary is *the SSH session itself*, not a scheduler hop. Two parties, one channel, synchronous, repeatable.

It also fits BUS0 vocabulary nicely: garrison's existing role is "workload account provisioning"; we're just expanding "provisioning" to include the per-user WSL distribution that the workload's `command=` directive depends on. No new verb, no schtasks dependency, no new mechanism class — same SSH transport, one additional step in the ceremony.

The redesign pace ₢A-AA2 is where this shape lands. ₢A-AA3 (the spike) becomes either the first dry-run of ₢A-AA2 or gets dropped entirely, depending on how confident we are that SSH-as-workload populates HKCU on `wsl --import` (I think confident enough to skip the standalone spike, but you decide).

Want me to reslate ₢A-AA2's docket toward this shape?

## Character
Design conversation (BUSJGW redraft) then mechanical implementation.
Locked by the spike pace's empirical outcome.

## Docket
Refactor garrison-w: workload owns its WSL distribution. Admin no longer
installs rbtww-main globally; garrison creates the workload's own WSL
instance from a seed tarball, registered in workload's HKCU\Lxss.

Design decisions (informed by spike):
- Profile bootstrap (operator-manual interactive logon vs automated).
- Seed tarball source (admin pre-stages? per-host bootstrap?).
- Fate of `passwd --lock` in step 3 (vestigial or load-bearing?).
- BUSJGW namespace framing: was "Windows + WSL-Linux"; now adds
  workload's WSL distribution registration as third namespace.

Implementation: garrison-w step structure update in bujb_jurisdiction.sh;
BUSJGW spec redraft to match; BUHJ handbook update for any operator-manual
prerequisites the spike surfaces.

Done when:
- BUSJGW reads coherently against implementation intent.
- bash -n green; tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh green;
  tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh green at baseline.
- Empirical end-to-end deferred to the empirical pace.

## Character
Design conversation (BUSJGW redraft + step-order rework) then mechanical
implementation.

## Docket
Refactor garrison-w: workload owns its WSL distribution. Admin no longer
installs rbtww-main globally; garrison opens an SSH-as-workload session
and drives `wsl --import` from inside that session, so registration
lands in bujuw_user's HKCU\Lxss naturally. Garrison conducts; workload's
own logon session executes — the identity boundary is the SSH session
itself, not a scheduler hop.

Structural shift in step ordering: the wsl-bound `command=` directive
can't be the first authorized_keys form (SSH-as-workload would route
through unimported wsl). New shape: bare authorized_keys →
SSH-as-workload imports rbtww-main + places privkey + validates →
replace with locked-down `command=` entry.

Design decisions:
- Seed tarball provenance (admin pre-stages via `wsl --export`?
  per-host bootstrap? committed/cached?).
- Fate of `passwd --lock` in step 3 — vestigial after cycle-9's profile
  registration landed, or still load-bearing for some Windows logon
  path?
- BUSJGW namespace framing: was "Windows + WSL-Linux"; now adds
  workload's WSL distribution registration as third namespace.

Implementation: bujb_jurisdiction.sh step structure update; BUSJGW
spec redraft; BUHJ handbook update for any operator-manual
prerequisites the redesign surfaces.

Done when:
- BUSJGW reads coherently against implementation intent.
- bash -n green; tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh green;
  tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh green at baseline.
- Empirical end-to-end deferred to the empirical pace.

The reslate failed — gallops parse error. The deserializer is asking for a top-level field `next_heat_seed` that doesn't exist in the JSON; the file has `jjgrn_next_heat_seed` (prefixed). This looks like a stale `vvx` binary versus a renamed schema, or a missing `#[serde(rename = "jjgrn_next_heat_seed")]` on whichever struct field maps to the seed.

Two read-paths (`jjx_orient`, `jjx_brief`) worked fine earlier; the write-paths (`jjx_validate`, `jjx_redocket`) hit it. So the gallops file itself isn't malformed JSON — there's just a binary↔file mismatch on that one field.

The reslate text is staged in `gazette_in.md`; rerunning `jjx_redocket` after the binary/schema sync will land it without further work from me.

Want to rebuild `vvx` via `tt/vow-b.Build.sh` and retry, or do you want to look at the rename state in `jjrt_types.rs` first?

content.text:
Dropped.

**Yes — plausible path, with one honest unknown.**

The proof chain so far:
- Cycle 9 established that Win32-OpenSSH builds a real workload logon session for bujuw_user once `HKLM\…\ProfileList\<SID>` is registered. That's the foundation: SSH-as-workload IS Windows' canonical "run this as that user" mechanism, synchronous, exit-code-honest.
- Microsoft documents WSL distributions as per-user (HKCU\Lxss). So the workload needs its own; we don't fight that.
- `wsl --import` is the supported, deterministic way to register a distribution for the current user from a tarball. No Store, no interactive flow, no scheduler.

**The one remaining empirical question**: does an SSH-as-workload session with *bare* authorized_keys (no `command=` constraint) drop us into a shell where `wsl --import` actually runs and writes to HKCU? Cycle-9 proved auth; we have not yet proven what happens *after* auth when the session isn't routed through the (currently broken) wsl-bound `command=`. The post-auth ssh exit 255 we saw before was likely the wsl invocation itself failing — but we don't know that for certain until we try the bare path.

**₢A-AA2's first execution answers this.** That's why it's not a separate spike — the redesigned garrison-w starts with bare authorized_keys → SSH-as-workload → `wsl --import`. The first run either populates HKCU (path confirmed, finish the redesign) or fails in a specific Windows way that tells us what the next constraint is. Either outcome converges.

**If it fails**: the failure mode dictates next steps — possibilities include LoadProfile not running for non-interactive logons, HKCU mounting differently for SSH sessions, etc. We'd insert a new pace at that point with concrete error in hand, rather than guessing now.

So: the path is solid in shape, with the final brick being something we'll know after one experiment that's already on the docket as ₢A-AA2.

file.content:
# Step 3 -- create the workload account fresh, ssh-only, no privilege.
zbujb_garrison_step3_create() {
  local z_letter="${1:-}"
  local z_wlu="${BUJB_workload_user}"
  buc_step "  [3/6] Create workload (${z_wlu})"

  case "${z_letter}" in
    b)
      case "${BURN_PLATFORM}" in
        bubep_linux)
          zbujb_admin_exec b                                              \
            "set -euo pipefail"                                           \
            "sudo -n useradd --create-home --shell /bin/bash '${z_wlu}'"  \
            "sudo -n passwd  --lock '${z_wlu}'"
          ;;
        bubep_mac)
          # Mac uses dscl/sysadminctl; left for in-environment refinement.
          # Operator may need to seat a more idiomatic primary group ID.
          zbujb_admin_exec b                                              \
            "set -euo pipefail"                                           \
            "sudo -n sysadminctl -addUser '${z_wlu}' -roleAccount"        \
            "sudo -n dscl . -create '/Users/${z_wlu}' UserShell /bin/bash"
          ;;
      esac
      ;;
    c)
      # Cygwin reflects Windows user accounts; mint via net.exe with a
      # disabled-password posture (we want ssh-key-only).
      zbujb_admin_exec c                                                  \
        "set -euo pipefail"                                               \
        "net.exe user '${z_wlu}' /add /passwordreq:no /active:yes > /dev/null" \
        "mkpasswd -l -u '${z_wlu}' >> /etc/passwd"                        \
        "mkdir -p '/home/${z_wlu}'"                                       \
        "chown -R '${z_wlu}' '/home/${z_wlu}'"
      ;;
    w)
      # Mirrored identity: net.exe creates the Windows user (SSH auth
      # boundary), useradd creates the Linux user inside WSL (execution
      # context). Same Windows-user shape as the c branch above; the
      # WSL-side useradd layered on top.
      zbujb_admin_exec w                                                  \
        "set -euo pipefail"                                               \
        "net.exe user '${z_wlu}' /add /passwordreq:no /active:yes > /dev/null" \
        "useradd --create-home --shell /bin/bash '${z_wlu}'"              \
        "passwd  --lock '${z_wlu}'"
      # OpenSSH-Win32 silently closes at preauth if the workload SID has
      # no HKLM\...\ProfileList entry. net.exe user /add creates the SAM
      # entry but not the profile registration. Win32-OpenSSH issue #1383.
      # Bash-orchestrated per WSG-PS-5: capture SID, build registry path
      # in bash, then two atomic single-expression PS calls.
      local z_sid z_regkey z_homepath
      z_sid=$(zbujb_powershell_capture zbujb_privileged "(Get-LocalUser '${z_wlu}').SID.Value") \
        || buc_die "step 3 (w): could not resolve SID for ${z_wlu}"
      z_regkey="HKLM:\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\ProfileList\\${z_sid}"
      z_homepath="%SystemDrive%\\Users\\${z_wlu}"
      zbujb_admin_powershell "New-Item -Path '${z_regkey}' -Force | Out-Null"
      zbujb_admin_powershell "New-ItemProperty -Path '${z_regkey}' -Name 'ProfileImagePath' -Value '${z_homepath}' -PropertyType ExpandString -Force | Out-Null"
      ;;
  esac

content.text:
Opening the design conversation.

I checked: `passwd --lock` is the Linux-side hardening (lines 714 + 745) — admin-side runs `useradd --create-home` then `passwd --lock` to enforce SSH-key-only on the WSL-Linux account. It's not Windows-related; the Windows-side blank-password gate is `passwordreq:no`. So that question reframes.

**Three decisions to settle, with my proposals:**

**1. Seed tarball provenance — proposal: admin pre-stages via `wsl --export` of their clean rbtww-main.**

Concretely: garrison-w's first new step is, from the privileged session, `wsl --export rbtww-main C:\Users\Public\rbtww-seed.tar`. Then SSH-as-workload imports from that path. Handbook discipline: admin is responsible for keeping rbtww-main pristine (operator-managed, not garrison-managed). Trade-off accepted: if admin customized their rbtww-main, drift propagates to workload — but this is test infrastructure, the operator can re-baseline.

Alternatives I considered and rejected: per-host bootstrap (slower, network dep), committed tarball (repo bloat), `wsl --install` from Store (interactive, network).

**2. `passwd --lock` fate — proposal: load-bearing, but moves locations.**

It's currently called inside admin's rbtww-main (since admin's WSL is the only one). Post-redesign, the Linux user lives inside *workload's* WSL distribution (the imported one), so `useradd` + `passwd --lock` move into the SSH-as-workload session that runs immediately after `wsl --import`. Discipline unchanged: workload's Linux account stays key-only.

**3. BUSJGW namespace framing — proposal: three namespaces, not two.**

Old framing was "Windows + WSL-Linux." Microsoft's per-user WSL design forces a third namespace: the workload user's WSL distribution registration (HKCU\Lxss). So BUSJGW prose needs:
- Windows workload account (SAM + profile + authorized_keys)
- Workload's WSL distribution registration (per-user, in HKCU\Lxss)
- WSL-Linux workload account inside that distribution (Linux SAM + locked password + authorized_keys for the inner shell, if relevant)

Mirror story stays clean: same `bujuw_user` name across all three.

---

**Plus the structural step-shape implied by the docket:** the existing step 4 (place workload pubkey with `command=`) splits — bare authorized_keys placed first, the locked-down `command=` form replaces it after SSH-as-workload has done its import + privkey + validate work. That much is forced by chicken-and-egg.

Anything you want to push back on before I start the BUSJGW redraft? My instinct is to redraft the spec first, then implement against the redrafted shape — but if you'd rather see implementation pseudocode against the current spec to test the shape, say so.

1	//axhob_operation
2	{bust_garrison_wsl}
3	
4	NOTE: {busn_garrison} ceremony with WSL bash as the workload shell,
5	for Windows nodes reached via the WSL distribution `rbtww-main`
6	(hardcoded in {bujb_jurisdiction}).
7	On Windows the workload identity inhabits *two namespaces*:
8	a Windows local user (created via `net.exe user /add`) provides the
9	SSH authentication boundary that Windows OpenSSH discovers, and a
10	Linux user inside `rbtww-main` (created via `useradd`) provides the
11	WSL execution context entered by the forced command. The Windows
12	user is the same shape minted by the c branch ({bust_garrison_cygwin}),
13	so Windows OpenSSH treats inbound auth identically; the WSL-side
14	`useradd` is layered on top so the SSH session lands as the named
15	WSL user (via `wsl.exe --user`) rather than the distro's default user.
16	Workload-only: garrison destroys any prior workload account named
17	{bujb_workload_user} via the letter-agnostic
18	`zbujb_obliterate_workload` helper in {bujb_jurisdiction} — the
19	single greppable destroy verb. The covenant scope spans every
20	namespace a prior garrison of any letter could have written to: the
21	Windows SAM entry, the `C:\Users\<user>\` Windows profile directory,
22	the `C:\cygwin64\home\<user>\` Cygwin home, and the WSL-side
23	`/home/<user>` inside `rbtww-main`. Each step is idempotent on
24	absent state.
25	Garrison never reads or writes any `sshd_config` and never modifies
26	admin authorized_keys; admin trust is established beforehand by
27	{busn_fenestrate}
28	on the Windows OpenSSH server, while sshd hardening inside the WSL
29	distribution remains operator-manual.
30	
31	//axhopt_typed_parameter
32	{burp_investiture}
33	
34	//axhoq_precondition
35	A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and its
36	referenced {burn_regime} profile is reachable.
37	
38	//axhoq_precondition
39	The WSL distribution `rbtww-main` (hardcoded as `BUJB_wsl_distribution`
40	in {bujb_jurisdiction}) is installed on the Windows host. Garrison-w
41	verifies this in a pre-flight after admin SSH succeeds; absence
42	surfaces a diagnostic naming the constant and emitting copy-paste
43	operator hints in the `tt/buw-jpS <investiture> '…'` shape rather
44	than letting an opaque `wsl.exe` failure escape downstream.
45	
46	//axhoq_precondition
47	{busn_fenestrate} has run for this {burp_investiture} so that
48	{burp_privileged_user} can authenticate by key alone using
49	{burp_privileged_key_file} into the Windows OpenSSH host (the admin
50	entry point); the WSL session is then opened from that foothold.
51	
52	//axhoq_precondition
53	{burp_workload_key_file} is unencrypted and exists on the operator's
54	station.
55	
56	//axhos_step
57	. *Open admin SSH*: connect as {burp_privileged_user} using
58	{burp_privileged_key_file} into the Windows OpenSSH host; key-auth
59	only. Failure here surfaces a missing or stale admin trust.
60	
61	//axhos_step
62	. *Pre-flight WSL distribution*: assert `BUJB_wsl_distribution` is
63	installed on the host (`wsl.exe --list --quiet` via the admin
64	session). Absence dies with a diagnostic naming the constant and
65	emitting `tt/buw-jpS` install hints; presence proceeds.
66	
67	//axhos_step
68	. *Obliterate workload*: invoke `zbujb_obliterate_workload`, the
69	letter-agnostic destroy helper. On Windows the helper executes a
70	single PowerShell session over admin SSH that purges the Windows
71	SAM entry (`net.exe user /delete`), the Windows profile directory
72	(`C:\Users\<user>\`), the Cygwin home (`C:\cygwin64\home\<user>\`),
73	and the WSL-side Linux user inside `rbtww-main`
74	(`wsl.exe --user root userdel -r`).
75	Each operation is idempotent on absent state.
76	
77	//axhos_step
78	. *Create workload*: provision {bujb_workload_user} in *both*
79	namespaces — the Windows user via `net.exe user /add /passwordreq:no
80	/active:yes` (matching the c branch) and the WSL Linux user via
81	`useradd --create-home --shell /bin/bash`.
82	
83	//axhos_step
84	. *Place workload trust*: write the workload pubkey
85	(derived via `ssh-keygen -y`) to the Windows-side authorized_keys at
86	`/mnt/c/Users/${BUJB_workload_user}/.ssh/authorized_keys` so Windows
87	OpenSSH discovers it natively (the SSH auth boundary is the Windows
88	user). The directory is created with `mkdir -p` and chmod 700; the
89	file is written and chmod 600. No POSIX `chown` is performed —
90	`/mnt/c/...` ownership is meaningless to Windows OpenSSH, which
91	inspects NTFS ACLs; the inherited NTFS ACL from the parent profile
92	directory is admin-owned and read-only for non-admins, satisfying
93	StrictModes.
94	The line carries the shell-letter `w` `command=` directive below —
95	locked spec content, mirrored at `BUJB_command_w` in
96	{bujb_jurisdiction}; `${BUJB_workload_user}` is substituted with the
97	canonical workload user at directive-emit time:
98	+
99	[listing]
100	----
101	command="C:/Windows/System32/wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user ${BUJB_workload_user} --exec /bin/bash -lc \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\"",no-port-forwarding,no-X11-forwarding,no-agent-forwarding
102	----
103	
104	//axhos_step
105	. *Plant workload key*: copy {burp_workload_key_file} to the WSL-side
106	workload home at the shell-letter `w` destination path hardcoded in
107	{bujb_jurisdiction} (`/home/${BUJB_workload_user}/.ssh/id_ed25519`),
108	so the Linux session reads it natively for outbound to GitHub.
109	
110	//axhos_step
111	. *Validate round-trip*: SSH as workload, run a no-op command, verify
112	exit 0.
113	
114	//axhog_guarantee
115	On success the node has exactly one workload identity named
116	{bujb_workload_user} mirrored across the Windows user (auth boundary)
117	and the WSL Linux user (execution context inside `rbtww-main`).
118	
119	//axhoc_completion
120	Exit 0 when the round-trip validation succeeds.
121	Non-zero on WSL distribution absence (pre-flight), admin SSH failure,
122	account-management failure in either namespace, key-write failure, or
123	workload validation failure.
